Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service: it dramatically reduces large documentation sets down to a small, highly relevant context that clients can feed into their own LLMs. Key points:

- ~90% context reduction (e.g., from ~500K tokens to ~5K optimized tokens) so LLMs get only the most relevant content.  
- Token budgeting and fitting content to the client’s max_tokens limit.  
- LLM-agnostic: clients use their own LLM (no vendor lock‑in).  
- No LLM inference costs for Snipara (pure compute service → high margins).  
- Enables accurate, cost‑effective LLM responses by supplying optimized context (the real differentiator vs. providing LLM responses).

Short answer — Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service: it finds, ranks, compresses and returns only the small, highly-relevant slices of a customer’s documentation so a client LLM can answer complex queries within tight token limits.

Key points:
- 90%+ context reduction (example: 500K tokens → ~5K highly relevant tokens).  
- Token-budgeting: fits returned context to the client’s max_tokens.  
- LLM-agnostic / no vendor lock-in: clients use their own LLMs (Claude, GPT, Gemini, local models).  
- No LLM inference costs for Snipara — they only run the context engine (search, embeddings, chunking, ranking).  
- Session persistence, smart chunking, and semantic + hybrid search to improve relevance and efficiency.

In short: Snipara’s differentiation is optimizing context so any LLM can work effectively and cheaply on very large document sets.

Short answer
- Snipara measures tokens with tiktoken (cl100k_base) and builds an optimized context by selecting document sections that fit within a user-specified token budget (e.g., max_tokens=4000). It ranks candidates by relevance, takes highest-relevance sections first (greedy), and stops when the budget is exhausted. If a section only partially fits, Snipara applies a sentence-aware smart_truncate so you don’t cut mid-sentence and marks that section as truncated.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. Input
   - Parameters: query, max_tokens (token budget), search_mode (keyword | semantic | hybrid), include_metadata, optional session_id, and optional custom budget/allocation.
2. Search & candidate generation
   - Searches using the chosen mode:
     - keyword search (fast),
     - semantic search (embedding-based),
     - or hybrid (fuse keyword + semantic).
   - Produces candidate sections with a relevance score.
3. Accurate token counting
   - Uses tiktoken (cl100k_base) to count tokens for each candidate section (accurate token accounting, not chars/words).
4. Ranking
   - Sections are ranked by relevance score (and any collection/category priority).
5. Greedy selection into the budget
   - Iterate sorted candidates (highest relevance first).
   - If a whole section fits (tokens_used + section_tokens <= max_tokens) → include it.
   - If it would exceed the budget but there is remaining capacity → include a truncated portion that fits (see smart_truncate).
   - Stop when budget is exhausted.
6. Smart truncation
   - Truncate to remaining tokens using the same encoder to measure size.
   - Truncation respects sentence boundaries (cut at ., !, ?), appends "..." and marks the section's metadata truncated: true.
7. Session & system context
   - Session context can be injected and is included (subject to a configured cap — implementation notes mention reserving up to ~20% of the budget for session context / system instructions).
   - System instructions may be auto-injected when building the context.
8. Budget allocation across sources/categories
   - Shared collections and project docs can be allocated portions of the budget. Defaults shown in docs:
     - Shared category defaults: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%.
     - Project-level allocation is prioritized (examples show project getting a sizable share, e.g., 40–60% depending on configuration).
   - The TokenBudget can be customized; allocation logic then enforces budgets per category/source and selects within each allocation.
9. Response & metadata
   - Each returned section includes: title, content, file path, line range, relevance_score, token_count, and truncated flag when applicable.
   - The response includes total_tokens used, truncated (overall), session_context (if included), suggestions, and search diagnostics (sections_scanned, search_time_ms).
10. Configurable & pluggable
   - You can customize budget allocations, opt in/out of shared context, and choose search mode. The behavior is deterministic and reproducible given the same inputs.

Why this design
- Accurate token accounting (tiktoken) avoids surprises when sending context to LLMs.
- Greedy by relevance maximizes usefulness per token.
- Category/project allocation ensures critical rules (e.g., MANDATORY) and project-specific docs get priority.
- Smart truncation keeps text readable and avoids LLM confusion from mid-sentence cuts.

Example (conceptual)
- max_tokens = 4000
- Candidate A: 1200 tokens, relevance 0.94 → include
- Candidate B: 800 tokens, relevance 0.78 → include (total 2000)
- Candidate C: 2200 tokens, relevance 0.70 → only 2000 tokens left → smart_truncate to fit 2000 tokens, mark truncated
- Final response: sections with token counts, total_tokens = 4000, truncated: true, plus metadata and suggestions

If you’d like, I can:
- Show a sample request/response JSON using the exact schema Snipara returns, or
- Explain how to customize TokenBudget allocations for your project.

Short answer
- Snipara enforces a token budget by counting tokens (using tiktoken cl100k_base), ranking candidate document sections by relevance, and greedily including the highest-relevance sections until the max_tokens budget is exhausted. If a section only partially fits, it is smart-truncated at a sentence boundary and flagged as truncated.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. Input
   - Main params: query (string), max_tokens (int, default 4000), search_mode ("keyword" | "semantic" | "hybrid"), include_metadata (bool).
   - Optional session_id/session_context can be included; session context may be injected (up to a configured fraction — docs note up to ~20% of budget).

2. Search & candidate generation
   - Depending on search_mode, the engine performs keyword search, semantic search, or fuses both to produce candidate sections with an initial relevance score.

3. Token counting
   - Snipara uses tiktoken (cl100k_base encoder) to count tokens precisely for each candidate section.

4. Ranking & greedy selection
   - Candidates are ranked by relevance score.
   - The engine iterates highest → lowest relevance, adding full sections as long as total_tokens + section_tokens <= max_tokens.
   - When a section would exceed the remaining budget, the engine attempts a partial include by calling smart_truncate() for the remaining token allowance.
   - Stop when budget is filled (or no more candidate useful sections).

5. Smart truncation
   - Truncates text to fit remaining tokens.
   - Truncation is done at sentence boundaries (., !, ?) to avoid cutting mid-sentence.
   - Adds an ellipsis ("...") to indicate truncation and sets section metadata truncated: true.
   - The truncated token_count reflects the tokens included.

6. Budget allocation across collections/categories
   - There are allocation strategies for shared collections vs project docs:
     - Project-specific docs are prioritized (examples show ~40% of total budget reserved for project docs).
     - Shared collections can be subdivided by category with default allocations (examples in docs show two sets of defaults):
       - Shared-category defaults (one representation): MANDATORY 0.4, BEST_PRACTICES 0.3, GUIDELINES 0.2, REFERENCE 0.1.
       - Global allocation including project (example): project 0.40, MANDATORY 0.25, BEST_PRACTICES 0.20, GUIDELINES 0.10, REFERENCE 0.05.
     - The engine supports custom budget_allocation overrides.
   - Within each bucket, docs are selected by relevance until the bucket’s allocated token sub-budget is used.

7. Output / metadata
   - The context query response includes:
     - sections: list of {title, content, file (path), lines [start,end], relevance_score, token_count, (optional) truncated}
     - total_tokens: tokens actually returned
     - truncated: whether the overall response hit the budget
     - session_context: previously stored context (if any)
     - suggestions: suggested docs to check
   - Additional telemetry: search_time_ms, sections_scanned, cache_hit, etc.

Other features & integrations
- System instructions can be auto-injected when building context.
- There are higher-level tools that use the same budgeting approach: multi-query (distributes a total budget across parallel sub-queries) and decomposition (estimates tokens for sub-queries).
- The engine exposes a tool rlm_context_query (params and response as above) and has Pydantic schemas for strong typing (ContextQueryParams, ContextSection, ContextQueryResult).

Why this matters
- Precise token counting + priority-based allocation lets Snipara provide the most relevant context within a fixed token budget, improving LLM prompt effectiveness and controlling cost. Smart truncation and metadata preserve usability and traceability of included content.

- Show a short pseudocode example of the greedy selection + smart_truncate algorithm, or
- Demonstrate how to craft an rlm_context_query request and interpret the response JSON.

Summary — how Snipara's token budgeting works

High-level idea
- Snipara treats the available token budget as a scarce resource and automatically selects the most relevant document sections to include in an LLM prompt, while guaranteeing you don’t exceed a specified max_tokens budget.
- It counts tokens precisely (using tiktoken with the cl100k_base encoder) and performs greedy selection by relevance. If a full section would overflow the budget it will smart-truncate the section at a sentence boundary and mark it as truncated.

Key parts & flow
1. API / tool
   - Primary tool: rlm_context_query
   - Main parameters:
     - query: str
     - max_tokens: int (default 4000) — total token budget for returned context
     - search_mode: "keyword" | "semantic" | "hybrid"
     - include_metadata: bool (include source file / line ranges etc.)
     - session_id / session_context may be provided (optional)
   - Response includes:
     - sections: list of selected sections (title, content, file path, lines, relevance_score, token_count, truncated flag)
     - total_tokens: tokens used by returned sections
     - truncated: whether the overall response hit the budget
     - session_context: injected previous/session context (if any)
     - suggestions: related docs to check
     - usage/search metadata (search_time_ms, sections_scanned, cache_hit)

2. Accurate token counting
   - Uses tiktoken (cl100k_base) to encode content and count tokens exactly (so counts match downstream LLM tokenization).
   - Each candidate section is measured in tokens before selection.

3. Selection algorithm (greedy by relevance)
   - Candidate sections are produced by the chosen search mode:
     - keyword search, semantic search (embedding-based), or hybrid (fuse results).
   - Candidates are ranked (relevance score).
   - Greedy inclusion: include highest-relevance sections first until adding the next full section would exceed max_tokens.
   - If a partial section can fit in the remaining token budget, the engine uses smart_truncate() to fit the remaining tokens and marks that section as truncated.

4. Smart truncation
   - When only part of a section fits, the engine truncates to fit the remaining token budget.
   - Truncation respects sentence boundaries (cuts at ., !, or ? where possible).
   - An ellipsis ("...") is appended and the section metadata includes truncated: true.
   - The engine ensures it doesn’t cut mid-sentence unless unavoidable.

5. Session & system injection
   - Session context (if available) can be injected into the returned context; the design reserves up to a portion of the budget (documentation notes a session context allowance ~20% of budget).
   - System instructions can be auto-injected when building the final prompt (and are accounted for in budgeting).

6. Budget allocation across collections/categories
   - Snipara supports budget allocation policies for shared collections vs project docs and by category.
   - Example allocation schemes shown in the docs:
     - Category-centric example: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%.
     - Frontend/default example (project-first): project 40%, MANDATORY 25%, BEST_PRACTICES 20%, GUIDELINES 10%, REFERENCE 5%.
   - These allocations are configurable; the system groups docs by source/category and applies per-category budgets, filling each group in priority order (by relevance) up to its allocated token share.

7. Hard limits & enforcement
   - There are guardrails such as MAX_COLLECTIONS_PER_PROJECT, MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION, MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION, and a MIN_RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD.
   - When a collection exceeds its per-collection token cap, documents are truncated/filtered to fit the limit.

8. Multi-query & decomposition support
   - The engine also supports decomposing complex queries into sub-queries (no LLM required) and running multiple context queries in parallel, each with their own per-query token budgets (multi-query execution splits budgets among queries).
   - Results from parallel/sub-queries are returned with per-query tokens used and aggregated totals.

9. Observability & outputs
   - The tool returns tokens used and search metrics (sections scanned, search_time_ms, cache_hit in some responses) so you can audit token-efficiency and compression ratios.
   - There are built-in measures (token_efficiency metrics) to estimate savings: compression_ratio, token_reduction_pct, cost_savings_pct.

Practical consequences for users / integrators
- Set max_tokens to control prompt size/cost: rlm_context_query(query, max_tokens=...)
- Prefer hybrid search_mode for best recall (fuses keyword and semantic).
- If you want project docs prioritized, configure budget_allocation to raise project share (defaults vary by component but are configurable).
- You will receive metadata (file path, line ranges, relevance) so callers can show provenance.
- The returned context will never silently exceed the budget; partial sections are clearly flagged as truncated.

Where this lives in the codebase (reference)
- rlm_context_query implementation in apps/mcp-server/src/rlm_engine.py (uses tiktoken get_encoding("cl100k_base")).
- Models (Pydantic) exist for ContextQueryParams, ContextSection, ContextQueryResult.
- Frontend/service allocation logic in apps/web/lib/services/token-budget.ts (allocateTokenBudget and limits).
- Tests: apps/mcp-server/tests/test_context_query.py (new test coverage).

If you want, I can:
- Show an example rlm_context_query request + a mocked response in the exact JSON shape the tool returns.
- Explain how to tune allocations for your project (recommendations for project vs shared collections and category ratios).

Short answer
- Shared context collections are team-level libraries of reusable documents (coding standards, security rules, templates, best practices) that are automatically injected into queries for any projects linked to the collection.
- They ensure every developer/agent receives the same non-negotiable rules and recommended patterns so implementations stay consistent across the team.

What they contain
- Documents organized into 4 categories (priority order):
  - MANDATORY (highest) — non‑negotiable rules (security, architecture)
  - BEST_PRACTICES — recommended patterns and conventions
  - GUIDELINES — helpful suggestions
  - REFERENCE — background/examples
- Each document has a category and priority (0–100) used for ordering/relevance.

How they work (end-to-end)
1. Create collection (team admin) and upload documents (rlm_upload_shared_document or Team UI).
2. Link the collection to one or more projects in the dashboard.
3. When you run a project query (rlm_context_query), the system loads shared context first and reserves part of the token budget for it. The remainder of the budget is used to query project-specific docs.
   - Within the shared context budget, tokens are allocated across categories (typical split: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%).
   - The overall shared vs project split is configurable; common defaults are in the 30–40% shared context range (project gets the remaining 60–70%).
4. Returned context contains merged shared documents (highest priority first) followed by project sections. You can inject the shared context into the session so subsequent requests always include it (rlm_inject).
5. Agents and tooling (e.g., RLM-Runtime) then implement features following the injected team standards; you can record the decision via rlm_remember.

Common API/tools
- rlm_list_collections(include_public=true) — find available shared collections
- rlm_upload_shared_document(...) — add docs to a collection
- rlm_shared_context(categories=[...], max_tokens=...) — fetch merged shared context
- rlm_context_query(query, include_shared_context=true, shared_context_budget_percent=...) — query project docs with shared context
- rlm_list_templates / rlm_get_template — templates from collections
- POST /api/teams/[teamId]/context — create a team context entry (admin)

Practical tips / best practices
- Put only non-negotiables into MANDATORY. Keep those short and authoritative.
- Use BEST_PRACTICES for patterns teams should follow but can be overridden in rare cases.
- Use priority scores to signal importance inside a category.
- Keep each document focused (one topic per document) and update them as standards evolve.
- Team plan required (Team or Enterprise) — not available on Free/Pro.

Example (high-level)
- Admin uploads “Coding Standards” with MANDATORY: “Always validate API input with Zod”.
- Project queries for “implement registration endpoint”.
- System injects the shared MANDATORY rule first (ensuring Zod validation is included) and returns combined guidance with project-specific docs.

If you want, I can:
- Show a concrete code snippet calling rlm_shared_context and rlm_context_query, or
- Walk through how to structure documents for MANDATORY vs BEST_PRACTICES. Which would you prefer?

Short answer
- Shared context collections are team-wide collections of documents (coding standards, security rules, architecture patterns, templates, etc.) that are linked to your team’s projects and automatically injected into MCP queries so the AI follows your team’s conventions.

How they work (conceptual)
- Team Admins create a Shared Context Collection and upload documents (rlm_upload_shared_document) categorized as MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES or REFERENCE.
- Projects that are linked to that collection automatically “inherit” those documents.
- When you run a query (rlm_context_query) the system merges shared context with project docs and returns shared context first so the model can enforce team rules before using project-specific material.

Key behaviors and rules
- Categories & priority: Documents are grouped and prioritized:
  - MANDATORY — highest importance (non‑negotiable rules: security, architecture)
  - BEST_PRACTICES — recommended patterns
  - GUIDELINES — helpful suggestions
  - REFERENCE — examples/background
- Token budgeting:
  - The shared context includes an internal allocation across those categories (typical example: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10% of the shared-context budget).
  - Separately, rlm_context_query gives you a shared_context_budget_percent (e.g., default 30% of the overall query budget goes to shared context, the remainder to project docs). So: overall → split between shared vs project; then shared content is split across categories by the category percentages.
- Query order: shared context is loaded first (so MANDATORY rules are applied), then project-specific documents are searched with the remaining token budget.
- Permissions & plan: Shared collections are a Team/Enterprise feature (not available on Free/Pro). Creating team contexts typically requires admin privileges.

Common API / workflow calls (examples)
- List collections (find available/shared/public collections)
  - rlm_list_collections(include_public=true)
- Upload a shared document
  - rlm_upload_shared_document(collection_id, title, content, category, priority)
- Retrieve merged shared context
  - rlm_shared_context(categories=[...], max_tokens=..., include_project_docs=true)
  - Returns documents + budget_allocation and total_tokens
- Query project context with shared context injected
  - rlm_context_query(query="...", max_tokens=..., include_shared_context=true, shared_context_budget_percent=30)
- Inject shared content into session so subsequent queries must follow it
  - rlm_inject(context="Team Coding Standards: ...", append=false)

Best practices for teams
- Put non‑negotiables in MANDATORY and keep them short and explicit (security, validation, architecture).
- Use priority (0–100) to rank documents inside a category.
- Keep documents focused (one topic per document) and update them as standards evolve.
- Provide templates (code review, PR checklist) in shared collections for consistent workflows.

Example usage scenario
1. Admin uploads “Coding Standards” with MANDATORY validation rules and BEST_PRACTICES for component structure.
2. Collections are linked to projects via dashboard.
3. A developer asks the AI to implement an endpoint. rlm_context_query runs, loads shared context first (applying MANDATORY rules like “use Zod validation”), then merges relevant project docs, and the returned response and generated code follow team standards.

Where to look next
- Use rlm_shared_context to preview what will be injected.
- Use rlm_context_query with include_shared_context and shared_context_budget_percent to control how much of the overall token budget goes to shared rules vs project docs.
- Admins can manage team contexts via the Team Context API (POST /api/teams/[teamId]/context) and the dashboard.

- Show a concrete sequence of API calls to create a collection, upload a document, link it to a project, and run a test rlm_context_query; or
- Summarize recommended content for a MANDATORY “Security” document your team could upload. Which would you prefer?

Short answer
- Shared context collections are team-level repositories of reusable rules, coding standards, templates and docs that get automatically merged into project queries so everyone’s AI responses follow the same team conventions.

How they work (conceptual)
1. Create a shared collection (team admin) and upload focused documents (coding standards, security rules, templates).
2. Link that collection to one or more projects in the dashboard.
3. When you run a project query (rlm_context_query), the system loads the shared context first and then the project docs with the remaining token budget.
4. The merged context is returned to the model and applied automatically — so implementations and suggestions follow your team’s rules.

Key behaviors and routing rules
- Shared context is loaded first and gets a configurable portion of the token budget (commonly 30–40% of the total query budget). The remainder is used for project-specific documents.
- Inside the shared context, documents are grouped by category and allocated a fixed share of the shared-context budget:
  - MANDATORY — highest priority (≈40% of shared-context tokens). Non‑negotiable rules (security/architecture).
  - BEST_PRACTICES — next (≈30%). Recommended patterns and conventions.
  - GUIDELINES — optional suggestions (≈20%).
  - REFERENCE — background/examples (≈10%).
- Documents have per‑category priority values (0–100) to order importance inside a category.
- Shared context is merged with project docs and returned with shared-context sections first, then local sections.

Common API / tooling you’ll use
- rlm_list_collections(include_public=true) — list available shared collections (team + public).
- rlm_upload_shared_document(collection_id, title, content, category, priority) — add a doc to a collection.
- rlm_shared_context(categories=[...], max_tokens=...) — fetch merged shared-context docs (optionally filter categories).
- rlm_context_query(query, max_tokens=..., include_shared_context=true, shared_context_budget_percent=...) — run a project query; shared context is included automatically for linked collections; you can control the percent of tokens allocated to shared context vs project docs.
- rlm_list_templates / rlm_get_template — use templates stored in collections (code-review, PR checklists, etc.).
- rlm_inject(...) — inject shared context into the session so every subsequent call sees it.

Typical workflow (practical)

Summary — how it works (step‑by‑step)

1. Client starts the device flow
   - The CLI/client calls POST /api/oauth/device/code with { "client_id": "snipara_cli" }.
   - Server responds with:
     - device_code (opaque string for polling)
     - user_code (short code user types, e.g., "ABCD-1234")
     - verification_uri (URL where user enters the code)
     - verification_uri_complete (URL that already includes the code)
     - expires_in (e.g., 900 seconds)
     - interval (polling interval, e.g., 5 seconds)

   Example response:
   {
     "device_code":"abc123...",
     "user_code":"ABCD-1234",
     "verification_uri":"https://snipara.com/device",
     "verification_uri_complete":"https://snipara.com/device?code=ABCD-1234",
     "expires_in":900,
     "interval":5
   }

2. User authorizes in the browser
   - The client shows the user_code and URL (or opens verification_uri_complete).
   - The user visits the page, signs in, and approves the client.
   - The server’s UI endpoint (GET /api/oauth/device/authorize?code=ABCD-1234) completes the authorization.

3. Client polls for the access token
   - The client repeatedly polls POST /api/oauth/device/token with:
     {
       "grant_type":"urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code",
       "device_code":"abc123...",
       "client_id":"snipara_cli"
     }
   - While waiting, the server returns errors like "authorization_pending" (and may return "slow_down", "access_denied", or "expired_token" per RFC 8628 semantics).
   - When the user authorizes, the token endpoint returns an access token (and refresh token):
     {
       "access_token":"snipara_at_...",
       "token_type":"Bearer",
       "expires_in":2592000,
       "refresh_token":"snipara_rt_..."
     }

4. Token usage and storage
   - The client stores the OAuth tokens locally (Snipara checks ~/.snipara/tokens.json first).
   - Use the access token for MCP requests via Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
   - Alternative: an API key can be used instead with X-API-Key: rlm_... (Snipara falls back to API key if no OAuth token).

Notes & useful bits
- The flow follows RFC 8628 (device flow): userless client obtains device/user codes, user authorizes in browser, client polls for token.
- Typical values in the docs: device_code expires_in = 900s (15m), polling interval = 5s; access token expires_in = 2,592,000s (30 days) and a refresh_token is returned.
- Priority for auth on MCP: check ~/.snipara/tokens.json (OAuth) first, then fall back to API key from environment.
- Troubleshooting:
  - If OAuth expired or not desired, either re-run device flow or remove ~/.snipara/tokens.json (or set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true) to force API‑key authentication.
  - If polling returns "authorization_pending", continue polling respecting the interval. If it returns "slow_down", back off.

Headers examples
- OAuth bearer token:
  Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
- API key:
  X-API-Key: rlm_your_key_here

Summary — how Snipara implements OAuth Device Flow (RFC 8628)

High-level flow
1. CLI/agent requests a device code from the server (POST /api/oauth/device/code).  
   - The client typically sends {"client_id":"snipara_cli"} (there is an optional auto_provision boolean to auto-create an account/project).  
   - Server responds with device_code, user_code, verification_uri (and verification_uri_complete), expires_in, and interval.

2. Show the user_code and verification_uri to the user (or open verification_uri_complete).  
   - User visits the verification URL in a browser and enters the user_code (or follows the complete URL) and signs in/authorizes the device.

3. Client polls the token endpoint (POST /api/oauth/device/token) using the device_code.  
   - Poll request body:
     {
       "grant_type": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code",
       "device_code": "<device_code>",
       "client_id": "snipara_cli"
     }
   - While the user hasn’t finished authorizing, the server returns a pending response:
     { "error": "authorization_pending" }
   - Poll at the interval provided by /device/code until success or expiry.

4. On success the token endpoint returns an OAuth access token (and refresh token):
   {
     "access_token": "snipara_at_...",
     "token_type": "Bearer",
     "expires_in": 2592000,
     "refresh_token": "snipara_rt_..."
   }
   - The client stores this token (Snipara clients use ~/.snipara/tokens.json) and uses Authorization: Bearer <access_token> for subsequent MCP requests.

Important fields & meanings
- device_code: short-lived server-side code used by the client when polling.
- user_code: code shown to the user to enter on the verification page.
- verification_uri: URL where the user enters the user_code (or signs in).
- verification_uri_complete: direct URL with the user code appended.
- expires_in (on /device/code): lifetime of the device/user codes (commonly 900s = 15m).
- interval: recommended polling interval (seconds).
- access_token: returned after authorization, prefix snipara_at_, used via Authorization: Bearer.
- refresh_token: used via /api/oauth/token to refresh the access token when expired.

Related endpoints & ops
- GET /api/oauth/device/authorize — page that shows the authorize UI (accepts code query param).
- POST /api/oauth/device/authorize — server-side authorize/deny action (UI/backend).
- POST /api/oauth/token — refresh an access token using a refresh token.
- POST /api/oauth/revoke — revoke a token.
- /api/settings/tokens — list and revoke user tokens.

Client behavior & precedence
- MCP clients check for a stored OAuth token at ~/.snipara/tokens.json first. If present, that is used. If not present (or ignored), they fall back to API-key authentication (X-API-Key: rlm_...).
- To force API-key usage instead of OAuth, either:
  - Set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the MCP client environment, or
  - Remove/backup ~/.snipara/tokens.json.

Troubleshooting tips
- If polling returns authorization_pending, keep polling at the recommended interval until user authorizes or the device_code expires.
- If you get an "Invalid or expired OAuth token" (HTTP 401) re-run device flow to get a fresh token.
- Use revocation endpoints to revoke tokens when needed.

Example (request device code)
curl -X POST https://snipara.com/api/oauth/device/code \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"client_id":"snipara_cli","auto_provision":true}'

Example (poll for token)
POST https://snipara.com/api/oauth/device/token
Body:
{
  "grant_type":"urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code",
  "device_code":"<device_code>",
  "client_id":"snipara_cli"
}

That’s the complete OAuth device-flow lifecycle as implemented in Snipara: obtain device + user codes, have the user authorize via the verification URL, poll for tokens, store/use the access token, refresh/revoke as needed.

Summary — what happens and the main endpoints

Snipara implements the OAuth 2.0 Device Flow (RFC 8628) so CLI/desktop clients can authenticate without copying API keys. High-level flow:

1. Client asks the server for a device code
   - POST /api/oauth/device/code
   - Request body: { "client_id": "snipara_cli" } (optional: auto_provision: true)
   - Response includes:
     - device_code (opaque token the client will poll with)
     - user_code (short code the user types)
     - verification_uri (URL the user visits)
     - verification_uri_complete (URL with code prefilled)
     - expires_in (e.g., 900 seconds)
     - interval (poll interval, e.g., 5s)

   Example response:
   {
     "device_code":"abc123...",
     "user_code":"ABCD-1234",
     "verification_uri":"https://snipara.com/device",
     "verification_uri_complete":"https://snipara.com/device?code=ABCD-1234",
     "expires_in":900,
     "interval":5
   }

2. User authorizes the device in the browser
   - User goes to verification_uri (or verification_uri_complete), enters the user_code (or uses the complete link), signs in, and approves the device.
   - The server provides a small authorization UI (GET /api/oauth/device/authorize?code=ABCD-1234) where the user can approve or deny the request.

3. Client polls for an access token
   - Client repeatedly POSTs /api/oauth/device/token while waiting for authorization:
     - Body: { "grant_type":"urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code", "device_code":"abc123...", "client_id":"snipara_cli" }
   - Typical polling behavior: wait the returned interval (e.g., 5s) between requests, stop when device_code expires (expires_in).
   - Possible responses:
     - Pending: { "error":"authorization_pending" } — keep polling.
     - Success: { "access_token":"snipara_at_...", "token_type":"Bearer", "expires_in":2592000, "refresh_token":"snipara_rt_..." }
       - Access token uses the prefix snipara_at_ and a refresh token is returned for renewing the access token.

4. Client uses and stores tokens
   - Access token sent as Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
   - Tokens are typically stored locally (e.g., ~/.snipara/tokens.json).
   - The server validates tokens by DB lookup + expiry checks; scopes are enforced.

Other useful points / operational details

- Token refresh and revocation:
  - Refresh: POST /api/oauth/token (standard refresh flow).
  - Revoke: POST /api/oauth/revoke with { "token": "...", "token_type_hint":"access_token" }.
- Authentication priority for MCP clients:
  1. If a stored OAuth token exists (~/.snipara/tokens.json), the client will use it first.
  2. Otherwise it falls back to using an API key (X-API-Key: rlm_...).
- Troubleshooting / alternatives:
  - If you want to force using an API key instead of OAuth, either:
    - Set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the client environment (makes the client skip tokens.json and use the API key).
    - Or remove/backup ~/.snipara/tokens.json so the client cannot find an OAuth token.
- Error behavior:
  - Polling returns authorization_pending until the user approves.
  - If the code expires or is denied you’ll get an error and must restart the device flow.

That is the complete device-flow lifecycle in Snipara: obtain device/user codes → user authorizes in browser → client polls for token → client stores and uses the access (and refresh) token.

Here are the main database models used in Snipara, with a short description of each and key relationships/fields:

Core models
- User — represents a user account (relations to owned/shared collections, team membership, projects).

Shared context / content models (newer additions)

- SharedDocument — a document inside a SharedContextCollection (title, slug, content, category [MANDATORY/BEST_PRACTICES/GUIDELINES/REFERENCE], tags, tokenCount, contentHash, embedding, priority).
- SharedMcpConfig — tool/configuration for a collection (enabledTools/disabledTools, defaultMaxTokens, defaultSearchMode, autoProvision JSON).
- PromptTemplate — prompt templates inside a collection (name, slug, prompt text, variables, category).

Link / junction model
- ProjectSharedContext — junction table between Project and SharedContextCollection (projectId, collectionId) with per-link configuration: priority, isEnabled, tokenBudgetPercent, enabledCategories, acknowledgedConflicts.

Snipara uses a strict layered (tiered) architecture — thin HTTP Routes on top, then jobs/orchestrators, services (external API adapters), a centralized repository layer for DB queries, and shared utils/types at the bottom.

Key layers (top → bottom) and responsibilities:
- app/api/ (Routes) — thin HTTP handlers: authenticate, validate (Zod), delegate to service/repository, return response.
- lib/jobs/ — orchestrators / processors (can be imported by routes).
- lib/services/ — external API adapters (lazy init, retry logic).
- lib/db/queries/ — repository layer: all Prisma DB access and permission checks; route handlers must NOT call Prisma directly.
- types/, utils/ — shared, pure helpers and types (must not import other layers).

Important rules:
- Import hierarchy is one-way: lower layers cannot import from higher layers.
- Route handlers must be thin (authenticate → validate → delegate → respond).
- All DB access must go through lib/db/queries (repository pattern).
- utils/types have no dependencies on other layers.

(You can see this summarized in the project’s “TypeScript Architecture Layers” / “Layer Hierarchy” documentation.)

Snipara uses a strict layered architecture (a variant of the classic Layered/Clean/Onion pattern) with a single permitted import direction and clear responsibilities for each layer.

Core layers (top → bottom)
- Routes (apps/web/src/app/api/) — HTTP entrypoints; must be very thin handlers that: authenticate (requireAuth), validate input (Zod), delegate to a service or repository, and return/handle errors.
- Jobs / Orchestrators (lib/jobs/) — long-running processors/orchestrators called from routes or workers.
- Services (lib/services/) — external API adapters and business logic that interact with third‑party APIs (use lazy initialization & retry patterns).
- Repositories (lib/db/queries/) — centralized database CRUD and permission checks; all Prisma access must go here (never call prisma directly from routes or services).
- Types & Utils (types/, lib/utils/) — shared, pure utilities and type definitions that DO NOT depend on any other layer.

Important rules
- Import direction is one‑way: higher layers (routes/jobs) can import lower layers (services → repositories → utils/types), but lower layers must not import from higher ones.
- Database queries live only in lib/db/queries/.
- Route handlers follow the prescribed thin pattern (requireAuth → Zod parse → delegate → successResponse / handleError).
- Services encapsulate external integrations (e.g., lazy client init and retry logic).
- Types and utils have no dependencies on other layers.

In short: a strict layered/clean architecture with Routes → Jobs → Services → Repositories → Types/Utils and a one-way import rule.

Short answer
- Snipara (RLM/Snipara context optimization) reduces a huge codebase to a small, relevant context bundle so your LLM only consumes what it actually needs. The docs show reducing ~500K tokens down to ~5K (roughly 100× fewer tokens) and supporting token-budgeted, hybrid (shared + project) queries and recursive decomposition for near-infinite docs.
- That means you can fit the needed context easily inside a 128K window and pay for far fewer input tokens — typically an order-of-magnitude (or more) cost reduction on LLM token billing and far fewer LLM calls.

How Snipara helps (concrete mechanisms)
- Token budgeting & greedy selection: rlm_context_query accepts max_tokens and returns the highest-relevance sections up to that budget (counts with tiktoken, smart truncation at sentence boundaries).
- Shared context + priority allocation: shared/team rules (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, etc.) are included first using a configurable percent of the budget, so essential rules are always considered without bloating project context.
- Hybrid search (keyword + semantic): finds more relevant material so you don’t waste tokens sending irrelevant text to the LLM.
- Smart truncation & metadata: sections truncated to fit remaining tokens (no mid-sentence cuts) and returned with file/line/relevance/token_count so you can audit or selectively expand.
- Session persistence: common context can be injected once and reused across multiple queries (reduces repeated input tokens).
- Recursive tools: For very-large/complex tasks, rlm_decompose + rlm_multi_query break questions into sub-queries and fetch compact context per sub-task — enabling effective handling of far more than the model’s window.
- No LLM inference on Snipara side: Snipara prepares the context; you still call your chosen LLM (so you control which model and pricing).

Why this matters for a 500K-token codebase + 128K window
- Without optimization: 500K tokens cannot be sent as a single input to a 128K-window model. You’d need chunking, many inference calls, stitching/synthesis, and a lot of duplicate tokens across calls — high cost and latency.
- With Snipara: You typically receive a condensed set of relevant sections (example from docs: ~5K tokens) that fits easily inside a 128K window with room for prompt + response. If you need deeper coverage, recursive sub-queries fetch only the necessary slices per sub-task.

Cost-savings (how to estimate)
- Basic formula:
  - Cost ∝ tokens_processed (inputs + outputs) × price_per_1K_tokens
  - Relative cost factor ≈ original_tokens / reduced_tokens
  - Percent saved ≈ 1 − (reduced_tokens / original_tokens)

- Example (illustrative):
  - Assume LLM input price = $0.02 per 1K input tokens (replace with your model’s price).
  - Sending 500,000 tokens once (hypothetical) = 500 × $0.02 = $10.
  - Sending 5,000 tokens = 5 × $0.02 = $0.10.
  - Direct saving = $9.90 (≈99% less input-token cost). Relative factor = 500k/5k = 100×.
  - Real world: you usually can’t send 500k at once, so you’d do multiple calls (e.g., 5 chunks) → multiply overhead and response tokens; Snipara avoids that overhead entirely in most cases, so effective savings are usually larger than the raw token ratio.

Other practical savings beyond token billing
- Fewer API calls → lower per-call overhead, lower latency, fewer round trips.
- Less engineering effort (no stitching/merge logic for chunked results).
- Better output quality: LLM receives higher-signal content (more relevant context).
- Governance & consistency via shared/team standards (reduces rework and safety issues).

Recommendations / next steps
1. Start by using rlm_context_query with max_tokens set to something that fits comfortably in your 128K minus estimated reply size (e.g., max_tokens = 10K or 40K depending on how much context you want).
2. Load team MANDATORY/BEST_PRACTICES into shared context once (rlm_shared_context) and inject as session context to avoid re-sending repeatedly.
3. For very broad tasks, use rlm_decompose to split into sub-questions and rlm_multi_query to fetch focused contexts in parallel.
4. Measure with tiktoken: run a few representative queries and measure tokens returned to compute exact cost savings against your LLM pricing.
5. Use the returned metadata to expand only the sections you need (selective expansion keeps token use low).

- Per-call LLM input cost with Snipara: cost_with = (Snipara_result_tokens / 1000) * P

- cost_per_call = (input_tokens / 1000) * price_per_1k_input_tokens

Example scenario:
- Raw corpus: 500,000 tokens
- Snipara result: ~5,000 tokens (example from docs)
- Reduction factor = 500,000 / 5,000 = 100×
- Percentage reduction = (1 − 5,000/500,000) × 100% = 99%

If your LLM charges (hypothetical) price_per_1k_input = $0.03 per 1K input tokens:
- Before Snipara: cost_per_call = (500,000 / 1,000) * $0.03 = 500 * $0.03 = $15.00
- After Snipara: cost_per_call = (5,000 / 1,000) * $0.03 = 5 * $0.03 = $0.15
- Savings per call = $14.85 (~99%)

If price_per_1k_input = $0.12 (more expensive model):
- Before: 500 * $0.12 = $60.00
- After: 5 * $0.12 = $0.60
- Savings per call = $59.40 (~99%)

Monthly/scale example (1,000 queries/month):
- At $0.03/1K: savings ≈ $14,850/month
- At $0.12/1K: savings ≈ $59,400/month

Compare that to Snipara plan cost
- Snipara plans in the docs: FREE, PRO $19/mo, TEAM $49/mo, ENTERPRISE $499/mo.
- Even at the top enterprise plan, the savings from reducing token usage are several orders of magnitude larger than the Snipara subscription cost in most realistic usage scenarios.

Other practical benefits (non‑monetary)
- Lower latency (smaller payload to the LLM).
- More predictable performance and fewer prompt engineering iterations.
- Ability to use smaller/cheaper context models or fewer large‑window LLM calls because the heavy lifting is done by the context optimizer and recursive orchestration.
- Traceability: returned metadata (file, lines, relevance) helps developers validate and reproduce sources.

Edge cases & notes
- The 500K→5K figure in the docs is an example; actual reduction depends on how much of the corpus is relevant. Typical reductions are “90%+” or better; your mileage will vary.
- Very complex end‑to‑end tasks may require multiple subqueries (rlm_decompose + rlm_multi_query). That still costs far less than naively feeding 500K tokens into the model each time.
- Session context can consume up to a configured part of the budget (docs mention up to ~20% in the implementation notes); account for that when setting max_tokens.

When to pick which (decision checklist)
- Use rlm_context_query when:
  - The question is a single, focused documentation request (e.g., “How does authentication work?”, “Where is function X implemented?”).

- Use rlm_decompose when:

1. Try rlm_context_query first (primary tool). If it returns a satisfactory, concise context → synthesize answer.
2. If the problem is large/complex or rlm_context_query returns too many scattered sections or truncated context, call rlm_decompose next:

   - Synthesize the sub-results into the final answer.

Short answer: use
- keyword for exact/technical lookups (function names, error codes, API endpoints),

Details and quick guidance

1) Keyword
- What it is: simple term/keyword matching.
- Pros: very fast, deterministic, great for exact matches.
- Use when: you’re looking for a specific function, config key, error code, API endpoint, or exact string.
- Example queries: "find function createUser", "error E1234", "GET /api/projects endpoint".
- Notes: available on Free plan and is the cheapest/faster option.

2) Semantic
- What it is: embedding-based similarity search (conceptual, context-aware).
- Pros: finds relevant content even when wording differs; good for conceptual, architecture, or “how it works” questions.
- Use when: you ask “How does authentication work?”, “Explain the architecture of X”, or want concept-level matches across docs.
- Example queries: "how do permissions and roles work?", "authentication flow for oauth".
- Notes: higher-quality for meaning, usually Pro+ (embedding) gated; slightly slower and more resource-intensive.

3) Hybrid
- What it is: combination of keyword + semantic (best of both).
- Pros: balances precision and recall — finds exact matches and conceptually-relevant results.
- Use when: general documentation searches, best-practices, broad research across docs, or when you don’t know whether your query needs exact or conceptual matching.
- Example queries: "best practices for database migrations", "how do we handle file uploads and S3 in this app?"
- Notes: recommended default for broad searches.

Practical tips
- If you know exact identifiers (function/class names, exact error codes) start with keyword.
- If you need understanding, background, or related concepts, use semantic.
- If unsure or doing research across docs, use hybrid.
- If you’re on Free plan and semantic/hybrid aren’t available, use keyword first; then broaden phrasing to improve recall.
- When in doubt: run hybrid (or semantic if hybrid isn’t available) — it generally returns the most useful set of results.

Below is a end-to-end trace of an MCP request starting in Claude Desktop and ending with a database query + the response back to Claude. I include both the common local-process (snipara-mcp bridge) path shown in your Claude Desktop example and the direct-HTTP path (alternate configuration). I call out the key code/files the request passes through and noteworthy behaviors (auth, tool dispatch, token-budgeting, errors).

High-level summary (one-line)
- Claude Desktop → MCP client/bridge (snipara-mcp) or direct HTTP → Snipara MCP server (FastAPI/stdio handler) → tool dispatch (rlm tools/services) → DB layer (db.py / models.py / services/*.py) → result assembled → JSON-RPC response → client → Claude Desktop.

Detailed step-by-step flow

1) Claude Desktop starts/addresses an MCP server
- In your example config Claude Desktop runs a command:
  - command: "uvx", args: ["snipara-mcp"]
  - env includes SNIPARA_API_KEY and SNIPARA_PROJECT_ID
- That causes Claude Desktop to spawn the snipara-mcp process and speak MCP (JSON-RPC) to it over stdio (local bridge).
- Alternate config: .mcp.json may specify an HTTP endpoint (e.g., https://api.snipara.com/mcp/<project-slug>) in which case Claude Desktop talks directly to the HTTP server instead of launching a local process.

2) Claude Desktop issues a tool request (JSON-RPC / MCP)
- Example: A tool call like rlm_context_query or tools/list sent as JSON-RPC:
  { "jsonrpc":"2.0", "id":1, "method":"tools/list", "params": {...} }
- Over stdio this is sent to the snipara-mcp process. Over HTTP the client encodes the same payload in the HTTP body.

3) snipara-mcp (local bridge / client) behavior
- snipara-mcp is the client-side package that bridges MCP requests to Snipara backend.
- It receives the JSON-RPC call on stdio, adds authentication headers (from SNIPARA_API_KEY or env), maps project ID (SNIPARA_PROJECT_ID), and forwards an HTTP request to the backend API endpoint (e.g., https://api.snipara.com/mcp/<project>).
- If snipara-mcp is used as a pure HTTP client (when installed and configured directly), it similarly signs the request and POSTs to the server endpoint.

4) HTTP request to backend (api.snipara.com/mcp/<project>)
- The bridge POSTs JSON-RPC to the server endpoint and includes auth (X-API-Key or Authorization header).
- You can test this directly with curl (docs include an example curl health / tools/list).

5) Entry on server: transport & auth (server.py / mcp_transport.py)
- The FastAPI/stdio server code (server.py, mcp_transport.py) accepts the request.
- mcp_transport.py validates the API key, project id and the JSON-RPC envelope.
  - If auth fails, mcp_transport.py / server.py return structured invalid-key/error messages (the codebase has explicit messages for missing/invalid keys).
- If valid, the server parses method and params, and hands the request to the MCP dispatcher.

6) Tool dispatch & request routing (tools / rlm_engine / services)
- The server maps JSON-RPC method names to tool handlers (examples: rlm_context_query, search, read, sections, decompose, etc.).
- The rlm-context tools implement token budgeting / semantic search logic:
  - rlm_context_query may perform multi-step retrieval: metadata search, section assembly, summarization, token-budget allocation.
  - First-query behavior: the server may inject tool tips (first_query_tips) on the first session call.
- Some tools call into higher-level orchestrators (rlm_engine / rlm-runtime) when execution or decomposition is needed (e.g., decompose, multi_query, or when a REPL is invoked).

7) Service layer → DB layer (services/*.py, db.py, models.py)
- When the requested tool needs persisted data (docs, sections, project config, user state), it calls the service functions which use the DB layer.
- Typical chain:
  - service function (services/*.py) builds a query,
  - uses models.py (ORM models) for shape and db.py for DB session/connection,
  - db.py executes the SQL/ORM query on the configured database (Postgres, etc.).
- The DB returns rows/records. Services may post-process (rank, filter, chunk) the data for the tool.

8) Optional compute / REPL / rlm-runtime
- For tasks that require computation rather than direct retrieval (e.g., parsing logs, running a small compute job, or assembling an optimized context), the server may call rlm-runtime or a local REPL to execute sandboxed code and return a compact result (the REPL returns a small JSON result rather than big raw logs).
- This step is used to reduce token usage and produce audited/safe computation results.

9) Server builds JSON-RPC result
- The tool handler composes the result payload (may include structured context, ranked sections, snippets, or summaries).
- The server enforces any token-budgeted formatting and returns the result as the JSON-RPC "result" field, or returns a JSON-RPC error if something went wrong.

10) HTTP response travels back to snipara-mcp (or directly back to Claude Desktop)
- If the client was the bridge (snipara-mcp), it receives the HTTP response and translates the server's JSON-RPC result into the stdio reply for Claude Desktop.
- If Claude Desktop spoke directly via HTTP, it receives the response directly.

11) Claude Desktop receives the tool output and continues the LLM flow
- Claude Desktop uses that tool output as context for the LLM (e.g., injects the returned sections and summaries into the prompt).
- The LLM (Claude) uses that enriched context to answer the user's question or continue the conversation.

12) Errors, logging, and diagnostics
- If auth fails or request malformed, server returns explicit invalid-key/auth messages (mcp_transport.py and server.py have specific checks/messages).
- For debugging, docs include curl examples to call /health or POST to /mcp/<project-slug>.
- Servers log the request/response lifecycle; rlm-runtime and orchestrator components log trajectories for auditing.

Files & components you’ll typically see touched in this flow
- Client-side: snipara-mcp (apps/mcp-server/snipara-mcp/), which runs as local bridge or HTTP client.
- Server-side entry points: apps/mcp-server/src/server.py and src/mcp_transport.py
- Tool implementations & orchestrator: rlm_engine.py (or equivalent tool registry), rlm_tools.py (RLM-compatible tool exports), services/*.py
- DB layer: db.py, models.py
- Optional runtime: rlm-runtime orchestrator / repl modules

Notes & gotchas
- Local vs HTTP: Claude Desktop’s config example runs snipara-mcp as a local process (stdio). If .mcp.json points to an HTTP endpoint, there is no local bridge — the client talks directly to api.snipara.com.
- First-query tips: the server may inject short tool tips on the first rlm_context_query call for a session.
- Token budgeting: rlm_context_query and related tools perform budgeted retrieval and may call multiple sub-tools (sections, read, search) to assemble a compact context.
- If you modify server code (apps/mcp-server/src/*), remember the dual-repo deployment requirement — these server files must be pushed/mirrored to the snipara-fastapi repo (docs/DEPLOYMENT_ARCHITECTURE.md notes).
- Pre-commit checks: run pnpm lint, pnpm type-check, and build locally before pushing changes.

  - stdio: client launches a local binary (e.g., `rlm mcp-serve`) and exchanges JSON-RPC over stdio.

    "method": "tools/rlm_context_query",

  }

- The transport layer reads API key (X-API-Key or Authorization Bearer) or project-specific header and calls auth logic.
- auth.py verifies:
  - API key validity
  - project membership / permissions
  - token expiration (if JWT)
- If auth fails: mcp_transport.py or server.py return an auth error (invalid API key/token messages referenced in docs).
- If success: the server resolves the project slug and loads project configuration (from DB via db.py or from config files).

- File(s): rlm_engine.py, services/*.py, models.py
- The dispatcher maps the JSON-RPC method (e.g., tools/rlm_context_query) to a tool handler in the tool registry.
- rlm_engine.py orchestrates tool execution, budgets, and sub-call handling (it may call the rlm-runtime orchestrator for complex decompositions/REPL work).
- For simple tools (search/sections/read) the handler may call services implemented in services/*.py which interact with DB and indices.

6) Tool execution: context retrieval and DB access
- If the tool needs project data, docs, memories, or indexes:
  - It queries the DB (Postgres) and caches (Redis) as needed.
  - File(s): db.py (DB connection and helper), models.py (ORM/models), services/*.py (business logic).
  - Example DB queries:
    - Load project metadata, templates, user preferences.
    - Read documentation index metadata (embedding ids, file refs).
    - Fetch agent memory rows (memories table).
  - If the tool involves semantic search:
    - The service will look up embeddings metadata, call the vector store (may be in DB or external store) and fetch candidate doc sections.
    - It then assembles sections and calls the token-budgeting logic (rlm_context_query) to produce an optimized context payload.

7) (Optional) REPL / code execution via RLM runtime
- If the tool requests execution (e.g., builtin execute_code, extract logs), the server may call the rlm-runtime integration:
  - rlm_runtime (or internal REPL) runs code in a sandbox (local RestrictedPython or DockerREPL).
  - The REPL returns a compact result (JSON) which the engine uses in the final response.
- File(s)/component: rlm-runtime integration (rlm_tools.py, orchestrator.py), rlm_engine.py.

8) Compose response (context optimization & token budgeting)
- File(s): rlm_context_query tool implementation, rlm_engine.py
- The tool composes:
  - The selected document snippets
  - Summaries or synthesized answer (if requested)
  - Metadata (which sections included, tokens used)
- Snipara applies token budgets and priority rules (80/20 pattern) to return the minimal, most relevant context rather than full documents.
- For first-query in session, Snipara may inject tool tips (first_query_tips_included) automatically.

9) Logging, telemetry, and auditing
- The server logs the request/response trace (trajectory logging if rlm-runtime is used).
- DB writes may record usage/quotas (usage.py) and audit info.
- Redis may store session context and caches for subsequent queries.

10) Transport: send JSON-RPC response back
- The tool result is serialized into a JSON-RPC response envelope:
  {
    "jsonrpc": "2.0",
    "id": 1,
    "result": {
      "content": "<optimized context / answer>",
      "metadata": { "sections": [...], "tokens_used": 450 }
    }
  }
- server.py / mcp_transport.py returns HTTP 200 with this body (or sends over stdio) to the client.

11) Claude Desktop receives the response and displays it
- Claude Desktop consumes the result and shows it in the assistant UI.
- Claude Code/Claude Desktop may run PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks (if configured) to further enrich prompts, track accessed files, etc. (see hooks in docs/.claude/settings.json).

12) Error-handling and fallbacks
- If the MCP HTTP server returns 5xx or times out, the client may fall back to local tools (read/grep/glob) as described in docs.
- Common error messages are emitted from mcp_transport.py and server.py (invalid key/token).
- Pre-commit and server-side checks (lint, type-check) are enforced for server code changes (developer note).

Deployment & infra notes (where it runs)
- Public MCP server: api.snipara.com (Railway, FastAPI)
- Repo layout: monorepo app code in apps/mcp-server/src/ — must be mirrored to snipara-fastapi repo for public deployment.
- Environment config: DATABASE_URL (Postgres), REDIS_URL, JWT_SECRET.
- Tool exposure: Snipara exposes 39 MCP tools (context_query, sections, search, read, shared_context, decompose, multi_query, etc.) via the tool registry.

Short answer — there are two authentication families supported, each with variants:

1) API keys
- Project API key (X-API-Key: rlm_...)
- Team API key (X-API-Key: rlm_...) — falls back if no project key

2) OAuth (device-flow) tokens
- Access token: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
- Refresh token: snipara_rt_... (token refresh endpoint available)
- CLI/browser device flow (RFC 8628) used to obtain tokens

Where they apply
- MCP server endpoints accept either X-API-Key (API key) or Authorization: Bearer (OAuth).
- Inject API (REST) endpoints accept X-API-Key only.

Technical notes and formats
- API key prefix: rlm_ (server stores a SHA‑256 hash of the key; lookup uses hash)
- OAuth access token prefix: snipara_at_ (server validates via DB + expiry)
- Refresh endpoint: POST /api/oauth/token (grant_type=refresh_token)
- Token management: GET /api/settings/tokens lists tokens; DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[id] revokes them
- Client behavior: CLI looks for ~/.snipara/tokens.json (OAuth) first, then environment API key; removing tokens.json forces API-key path

Security differences (pros / cons and recommended use)
- OAuth (device flow)
  - Pros:
    - Short‑lived access tokens with refresh token lifecycle → limits exposure if leaked.
    - Central revocation and expiry; easier to rotate and revoke per-device tokens.
    - Device flow avoids copying permanent keys into ephemeral clients (more user-friendly & secure for CLIs).
    - Scopes can be enforced server-side.
  - Cons / risks:
    - Refresh tokens are long‑lived and must be protected (stored by client, e.g., ~/.snipara/tokens.json).
    - If the client stores tokens in an insecure location, compromise of that file yields access.
- API keys
  - Pros:
    - Simple to use (header only), convenient for CI/CD, scripts, and server-to-server calls.
    - Server stores only a hash (SHA‑256) of the key — raw key is not kept in DB.
    - Team vs project keys provide flexible scope (project key = narrower scope).
  - Cons / risks:
    - Typically long‑lived and static unless actively rotated — greater exposure window if leaked.
    - Team keys are broader in scope (works across projects) — more powerful, therefore higher risk if leaked.
    - Keys may be accidentally leaked in environment variables, logs, or repository files.
    - API keys are sent in requests as-is, so intermediaries or misconfigured logging can leak them.

Practical security recommendations
- Prefer OAuth device flow for interactive users and local tools (short-lived tokens + revocation).
- For automation/CI, use project-scoped API keys where possible rather than team keys (least privilege).
- Rotate and revoke keys/tokens regularly; revoke unused tokens via DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[id].
- Store credentials securely (secrets manager, OS keychain, not plaintext in repos). Protect ~/.snipara/tokens.json or remove it to force API-key usage if desired.
- Audit usage and limit exposure: show only partial key in logs/UI, and enforce scopes on tokens.
- Use HTTPS endpoints (provided) to protect tokens in transit.

Example header usage
- API key (MCP or Inject): X-API-Key: rlm_your_key
- OAuth (MCP): Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_your_token

Summary — available authentication methods
- API keys
  - Project API key (rlm_...): scoped to a single project.
  - Team API key (rlm_...): works for all projects in a team (fallback if no project key found).
- OAuth tokens (Device Flow / Bearer tokens)
  - Access token (snipara_at_...): Bearer token used in Authorization header.
  - Refresh token (snipara_rt_...): used to obtain new access tokens.
  - Device flow (RFC 8628) for CLI/browser login (no manual API key copy).
- Client-side/local fallback/priority behavior
  - Client will prefer stored OAuth token (~/.snipara/tokens.json) and fall back to an API key (e.g., env var) if no OAuth token is present.
- Endpoint differences
  - MCP endpoints (api.snipara.com/mcp/{slug}) accept either Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_... or X-API-Key: rlm_...
  - Inject API (www.snipara.com/api/projects/{slug}/automation/inject) uses X-API-Key: rlm_... only.

How they differ in security (key points)
- Secret format & server storage
  - API keys: client holds a static secret beginning with rlm_. Server stores a SHA‑256 hash of the key (not the raw key) and looks up by hash. This protects server-side storage if DB leak occurs.
  - OAuth tokens: access tokens start with snipara_at_ and are validated by DB lookup + expiry. Refresh tokens allow obtaining new access tokens without re-authenticating.
- Lifetime & revocation
  - API keys: typically long‑lived until revoked/rotated. To rotate you must create a new key and update clients.
  - OAuth access tokens: short/medium lived (expires_at shown in tokens list). They can be refreshed using refresh tokens; tokens can be revoked via the API (DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[id]).
- Scope & principle of least privilege
  - OAuth: usually supports scopes and finer-grained enforcement (read/write scope enforcement noted in docs).
  - API keys: access is by project or team; less granular scope control than OAuth in many flows.
- Exposure risk & intended use
  - OAuth device flow: safer for interactive use because users authenticate in a browser without copying keys; minimizes accidental leakage and supports central revocation and rotation.
  - API keys: convenient for automation/CI and server-to-server calls but riskier if embedded in code or container images. Team keys give broad access — higher blast radius if leaked.
- Storage on the client
  - OAuth tokens may be stored locally (e.g., ~/.snipara/tokens.json) — convenient but a local compromise can reveal tokens; you can delete that file to force API-key use.
  - API keys in environment variables are common for CI, but ensure secrets aren’t checked into source control.
- Detection & error handling
  - Invalid/expired OAuth tokens return 401 with guidance to re-authenticate.
  - Invalid API key responses and recommended action: generate a new key from dashboard.

Practical recommendations (security best practices)
- For interactive users: prefer OAuth device flow — short lived tokens, central revocation, scope control.
- For automation/CI: prefer per-project API keys (not team keys) stored in secret managers / env vars; rotate keys periodically.
- Minimize blast radius: avoid using team API keys unless necessary.
- Revoke/rotate compromised keys or tokens immediately (DELETE token endpoint / dashboard).
- Don’t commit keys/tokens to source control; use secret stores and environment variables.
- Use HTTPS endpoints (all API traffic should be over TLS).

Short answer
- The system splits the available token budget between project docs and shared context, then further splits the shared-context portion by document category (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE).
- Within each bucket, documents are included in descending relevance until that bucket’s token budget is exhausted; any docs that don’t fit are marked “exceeded budget.”
- The exact percentages are configurable, but the runtime defaults are defined in the token-budget code.

Details (how it actually works)

1) Two-layer split: project vs shared
- When you run rlm_context_query the engine merges shared collections with project docs.
- Tokens are allocated across two layers: project documents (one bucket) and shared-context documents (split into category buckets).
- Shared context is loaded first (configurable), then project docs consume the remaining budget.

2) Default allocation values (runtime authoritative)
- The token-budget implementation (apps/web/lib/services/token-budget.ts) uses DEFAULT_ALLOCATION by default:
  - project: 40% (project-specific docs)
  - MANDATORY: 25%
  - BEST_PRACTICES: 20%
  - GUIDELINES: 10%
  - REFERENCE: 5%
- Those numbers sum to 100% (project 40% + shared categories 60%). This is the allocation used by allocateTokenBudget unless you pass a customAllocation override.

3) Alternate/Example percentages shown elsewhere
- Other docs and examples show different splits in different contexts:
  - rlm_shared_context examples present category-only splits like MANDATORY 40% / BEST_PRACTICES 30% / GUIDELINES 20% / REFERENCE 10% (this is the shared-context-only breakdown used by rlm_shared_context responses).
  - rlm_context_query examples show a 60% project / 40% shared split or use a parameter shared_context_budget_percent (e.g., 30%). These are illustrative and configurable options.
- Key point: you may see different numbers in docs/examples; the runtime code (token-budget.ts) and request parameters (shared_context_budget_percent, customAllocation) determine what actually runs.

4) How docs are chosen inside each bucket
- Documents are sorted by relevanceScore (if available) and then included one-by-one until the bucket token budget is used.
- If a doc would push tokens over the bucket limit it is marked allocated: false with reason "exceeded budget".
- The allocateWithinBudget function enforces this behavior.

5) Hard limits and collection enforcement
- There are hard limits you should be aware of (from the same file):
  - MAX_COLLECTIONS_PER_PROJECT
  - MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION (100,000)
  - MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION
  - MIN_RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD
- If a collection exceeds the per-collection token limit, enforceCollectionLimits will truncate the collection to fit before allocation.

6) Customization
- You can override defaults:
  - Pass a customAllocation to allocateTokenBudget.
  - Use rlm_context_query params like include_shared_context and shared_context_budget_percent when making queries.
  - rlm_shared_context can be called with categories and max_tokens to control returned shared content.

Recommended quick checks
- To know what will actually be used in your environment, check:
  - The rlm_context_query call parameters (shared_context_budget_percent, include_shared_context).
  - The DEFAULT_ALLOCATION and any customAllocation logic in apps/web/lib/services/token-budget.ts.

Short answer
- When you run a context query, the system splits the total token budget between shared-context documents and project (local) documents, then further splits the shared-context portion across the shared-document categories (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE). Shared context is loaded first, then project docs are filled with the remaining budget. Documents are included in each bucket in relevance order until the bucket’s token budget is exhausted.

How it works (details)

1. Two-level allocation (shared vs project)
- The query first allocates a portion of the total token budget to shared context and then uses the remaining tokens for project-specific docs.
- By default the system treats shared context as a separate budget slice (example default shown in docs: shared_context_budget_percent = 30 → shared gets 30% of total, project gets the other 70). This split is configurable per query.

2. Shared-context category split
- The shared-context budget is further split across the four document categories by priority:
  - MANDATORY (highest)
  - BEST_PRACTICES
  - GUIDELINES
  - REFERENCE (lowest)
- Conceptual/default category proportions shown in the docs: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10% (these percentages apply to the shared-context slice).
  - Example: totalBudget = 4000, shared_percent = 30% → shared_budget = 1,200. Then MANDATORY = 480 tokens (40% of 1,200), BEST_PRACTICES = 360, GUIDELINES = 240, REFERENCE = 120.

3. Document selection inside each bucket
- Within each bucket (project or a shared category), documents are sorted by relevance (and priority) and included until the bucket’s token budget is used up.
- Each document has a tokenCount; documents that would exceed the bucket are marked “not allocated / exceeded budget.”
- The allocation logic is implemented in allocateWithinBudget: it picks docs by relevance, keeps a running tokensUsed, and stops when the budget is reached.

4. Configurability & overrides
- You can adjust the shared/project split when calling rlm_context_query (e.g., shared_context_budget_percent).
- The allocateTokenBudget function accepts customAllocation to override the default allocation values.
- rlm_shared_context also accepts category filters and max_tokens.

5. Hard limits & safeguards
- There are hard limits (examples in the code): MAX_COLLECTIONS_PER_PROJECT, MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION, MIN_RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD.
- If a collection exceeds MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION, enforceCollectionLimits truncates/filters documents from that collection to fit the limit.

Notes about differing defaults in the docs
- The higher-level docs (and examples) describe the shared-first flow with the shared-context slice then split 40/30/20/10 across categories.
- There is also an implementation file (token-budget.ts) that shows a DEFAULT_ALLOCATION object where project gets 40% and the shared categories add up to 60% (project:0.40, MANDATORY:0.25, BEST_PRACTICES:0.20, GUIDELINES:0.10, REFERENCE:0.05). The important points remain the same: allocation is split across project vs. shared and then by shared-category priority, and these values can be customized by configuration or query parameters.

Practical takeaway
- Expect shared context to be loaded first and to consume a fixed slice of the total tokens, with that slice prioritized according to MANDATORY → BEST_PRACTICES → GUIDELINES → REFERENCE.
- If you need different behavior, pass a custom shared_context_budget_percent or custom allocation via the APIs (rlm_context_query / allocateTokenBudget).

Short answer
- When you query with shared context, the system splits the available token budget between project docs and the linked shared collections, then allocates the shared portion among the shared-document categories (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE). Shared context is loaded first, then project docs are queried with the remaining budget. Documents are included until each category’s budget is exhausted.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. Top-level split (project vs shared)
   - The total token budget is divided between project-specific documents and shared collections. Shared context is loaded first; project docs use the remaining tokens.
   - This top-level split is configurable (examples in docs show 60% project / 40% shared, another example uses 70/30). You can set this via parameters like shared_context_budget_percent when calling rlm_context_query or via project settings.

2. Shared-context breakdown by category
   - The shared portion is further allocated across categories. Typical category allocations shown in the docs:
     - MANDATORY (highest priority) — e.g. 40% of the shared budget (or the system examples vary)
     - BEST_PRACTICES — e.g. 30%
     - GUIDELINES — e.g. 20%
     - REFERENCE — e.g. 10%
   - In the implementation code (token-budget.ts) there’s another DEFAULT_ALLOCATION that splits the entire budget as:
     - project: 40%
     - MANDATORY: 25%
     - BEST_PRACTICES: 20%
     - GUIDELINES: 10%
     - REFERENCE: 5%
   - Note: the docs therefore show two related views — a) a conceptual category-percentage model for shared docs, and b) the actual allocation object used by allocateTokenBudget which divides the whole budget into project + each shared category. The effective numbers you see depend on your project’s configuration/version.

3. Per-category selection logic
   - For each bucket (project or each shared category) the system:
     - Sorts documents by relevanceScore (if present) and by document priority,
     - Adds documents until the bucket’s token budget is exhausted,
     - Marks documents that don’t fit as unallocated / “exceeded budget”.
   - This is done by allocateWithinBudget in the token-budget implementation.

4. Enforced limits and overrides
   - Hard limits exist (e.g., MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION) — large collections are truncated to fit limits.
   - You can override allocations programmatically:
     - rlm_context_query supports parameters like include_shared_context and shared_context_budget_percent.
     - allocateTokenBudget accepts a customAllocation to tweak the percentages used.
   - The Project Shared Context UI also lets you reorder linked collections and set per-collection tokenBudgetPercent in some setups, which affects priority/allocated tokens.

5. Result & reporting
   - rlm_shared_context and rlm_context_query return the selected documents plus a budget_allocation summary showing how many tokens were allotted to each category (example response includes numbers for MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE).

Practical implications / recommendations
- MANDATORY rules get highest priority and are included first; make sure critical rules are marked MANDATORY so they aren’t dropped.
- If you need more project context, increase the project share (shared_context_budget_percent) or reduce the shared-category percentages.
- Check your project’s DEFAULT_ALLOCATION (or the rlm_context_query parameters) to know the exact numbers in your environment — the docs show examples but the deployed defaults can vary.

Short answer
- If rlm_context_query finds no matching project docs it returns a successful response with no content sections (an empty "sections" array), total_tokens = 0 (or only shared-context tokens if shared context was included), truncated = false, plus session_context and any generated suggestions.  
- If the request fails for other reasons you may instead get an error HTTP response (examples in the docs: a 404 "Context not found" or a 429 rate-limit error).

What the tool actually returns (behavior)
- Success-but-empty:
  - data.sections = [] (or only the shared-context sections if include_shared_context was used and those exist)
  - data.total_tokens = 0 (or the number of shared-context tokens returned)
  - data.truncated = false
  - data.session_context may be present
  - data.suggestions = [helpful next steps] (the engine runs _generate_suggestions when it has no strong matches)
- Error cases:
  - If the engine cannot find the requested resource or there is an API-level problem you may get an error payload instead, e.g.:
    - 404 Not Found: {"error": {"code":"NOT_FOUND","message":"Context not found"}}
    - 429 Too Many Requests: returned when limits are exceeded (example shown in docs)

Why that happens
- The context query searches shared best-practices first then project docs (MCP Query Order). If neither shared nor project content matches the query, there simply aren’t any candidate sections to return.
- If the project has not been indexed/uploaded (or your query terms are too specific), the search will come up empty.

Suggested next steps / troubleshooting
1. Try broader or different keywords (remove stopwords, use core concepts or filenames).
2. Include shared context or increase its budget: use include_shared_context=true or adjust shared_context_budget_percent — shared context may contain mandatory rules or patterns that help.
3. Use rlm_search for regex/file-name lookups to detect whether documents exist.

Troubleshooting checklist
1. Indexing: Make sure your docs are indexed via the Snipara dashboard (upload .md/.mdx/.txt).
2. File types & paths: Confirm supported extensions and that path filters include your files.
3. Branch / sync: Ensure the repo branch matches (main vs master) and sync is enabled.
4. Permissions: Verify team membership / access — a 403 will appear if you're not a team member.
5. Network / tokens: Confirm any required tokens or API keys are set for syncing.
6. Search strategy: Widen the query (use synonyms or broader terms) or try hybrid/semantic search.
